claim: The 14-item header pool identified in H-BV-GALLOWS-STRIP-HEADER-01 (chor, ol, shol, cho, or, cheol, aiin, chol, odaiin, cheody, chody, chocthy, shor, eol) — which recur at Hand A paragraph-initial positions with gallows-prefix decoration — exhibits phonotactic and structural properties compatible with a Basque medical/herbal vocabulary pool. If compatible: Brain-V has a candidate for Basque section-header vocabulary; if not: the Basque-like reading does not extend to Hand A's topic/header layer.
evidence_for: If Hand A is Basque-like under Latin scribal abbreviation, the recurrent header stems should be a small, closed semantic class (plant types, body parts, pharmaceutical operations) following the same phonotactic rules as the rest of the Hand A stem inventory.

14 recurrent stems is roughly compatible with the size of a typical herbal section-header vocabulary (see Circa Instans: 15-20 principal section heads for plant families; typical pharmaceutical antidotarium: 10-25 operation types).

The header stems are paragraph-initial by construction (from GALLOWS-STRIP-HEADER-01), matching the structural role of Basque-language herbal catalog entries where each plant/operation would anchor its own paragraph.
evidence_against: No extant 15th-c. Basque medical/herbal text is available to Brain-V as a reference corpus. Brain-V's earliest substantial Basque attestation is Bernat Etxepare's 'Linguae Vasconum Primitiae' (1545), and earlier fragments (glosses, oaths) are lexically non-medical. Without medical-register reference, a direct vocabulary match is effectively untestable — only structural compatibility can be assessed.
Even if Hand A is Basque, the headers may be opaque compounds, proper names (plant species names in Latin transliteration), or abbreviations that do not obey regular Basque phonotactics.
The header pool size (14) is small; any phonotactic test on 14 items has low statistical power. Absence of signal is not evidence of absence.
test:
LOCKED PROCEDURE.

  DATA: The 14-item stripped header pool from H-BV-GALLOWS-STRIP-HEADER-01:
    {chor, ol, shol, cho, or, cheol, aiin, chol, odaiin, cheody, chody, chocthy, shor, eol}

  GLYPH PHONOLOGICAL CATEGORIES (LOCKED, imported from H-BV-BASQUE-PHONOTACTIC-01):
    VOWEL-like: {i, e, o, a}
    CONSONANT-like: {k, t, p, d, l, m, n, r, s, q, f, g, v, x, y} plus ligatures {ch, sh, ckh, cth, cph, ol}

  FIVE HEADER-POOL PROPERTIES (LOCKED):

  H1 — NO R-INITIAL:
    Number of header stems starting with 'r'. Basque predicts zero.
    PASS if count == 0.

  H2 — LENGTH DISTRIBUTION:
    Compute mean and median length (in glyph-units) of header stems. Basque category/topic words (plant names, body parts, operations) are typically 3-7 glyph-units. The stripped headers should fall in a plausible Basque content-word-length range.
    PASS if mean length in [2.5, 6.0] AND median in [2, 6].

  H3 — VOWEL-FINAL COMPATIBILITY:
    Fraction of header stems whose final glyph-unit is vowel-like. Basque common nouns often end in vowels (especially -a). Medical/herbal category terms in Basque include: belar 'grass/herb' (cons-final); lore 'flower' (vowel-final); hosto 'leaf' (vowel-final); zuhaitz 'tree' (cons-final). Mixed expectation — not a strong prediction.
    PASS if >=0.35 of headers are vowel-final (lower bar than general Hand A — categories can be cons-final).

  H4 — STEM DIVERSITY:
    14 header stems should be reasonably diverse (not all variants of one root). Compute: Levenshtein/edit-distance-based cluster count. Group stems whose pairwise edit distance is <=1. Basque section vocabulary for a coherent herbal would have at least 8 distinct clusters (each plant family, or each operation type).
    PASS if cluster count (at edit distance <=1) >= 8.

  H5 — CONSISTENCY WITH HAND A STEM PROFILE:
    The 14 headers should be drawn from Hand A's general stem inventory — they should not be outliers. Check: for each header, does it appear as a non-paragraph-initial form elsewhere in Hand A (possibly with additional outer suffixes)?
    PASS if >=70% of headers have at least one non-header occurrence in Hand A (with any Layer-2 outer).

  DECISION RULE (LOCKED):
    CONFIRMED: >=3/5 properties PASS. Headers are Basque-compatible structurally.
    MARGINAL: 1-2/5 PASS. Headers have some Basque-compatible properties but not systematically.
    REFUTED: 0/5 PASS. The header pool is structurally incompatible with a Basque herbal category vocabulary.

  CAVEAT (locked): without a Basque medical/herbal corpus to compare against, this test can only falsify via structural incompatibility. It cannot CONFIRM lexical match — a PASS means 'consistent with Basque', not 'identified as Basque'.
test_threshold: CONFIRMED if >=3/5 properties pass. MARGINAL if 1-2/5. REFUTED if 0/5.

tests_run.details:
CONFIRMED 3/5 at threshold, with one notable failure that is itself informative.

  RESULTS:
    H1 No r-initial:             0 r-initial / 14  PASS
    H2 Length: mean 3.07, median 3                  PASS
    H3 Vowel-final >=35%:         1/14 = 7.1%       FAIL
    H4 Clusters (edit-dist <=1) >=8: 4 clusters     FAIL
    H5 Hand-A consistency >=70%:  14/14 = 100%      PASS

  CLUSTER STRUCTURE (H4):
    Cluster 1 (9 members): chor, shor, or, cho, chol, eol, cheol, shol, ol
    Cluster 2 (3 members): cheody, chody, chocthy
    Cluster 3 (1 member):  aiin
    Cluster 4 (1 member):  odaiin

  The 9-member giant cluster is the most informative finding. All 9 members are within edit-distance 1 of at least one other member. Concretely:
    'ol' → add ch → 'chol' → add or → 'chor' → substitute ch→sh → 'shor' → etc.
  The cluster represents a small number of underlying roots (perhaps 1-3) expressed with varying prefixes and inflectional endings. This is morphologically CONSISTENT with an agglutinative language where paragraph-initial headers are case-marked forms of a single recurring category word (e.g., 'plant-NOM', 'plant-ERG', 'plant-GEN') rather than distinct section names.

  H3 FAILURE analysis: only 'cho' is vowel-final (1/14 = 7%). All other headers end in ligatures (ol), consonants (r, n), or glyphs categorised as consonant-like (y). This is unusual for Basque content nouns (which often end in vowels due to -a definite article) but could match case-marked forms: Basque ergative -k, locative -n, ablative -tik, etc. all end in consonants. If the headers are CASE-MARKED rather than bare, consonant-final is expected.

  H5 is strong (14/14): every header pool item has Hand-A occurrences either as the exact token (appearing elsewhere in non-paragraph-initial positions) or followed by a Layer-2 outer (chor+y, chor+n, etc.). For example, 'chor' appears 167 times as exact token and 10 times with a Layer-2 outer in Hand A. This means the headers are NOT isolated paragraph-marker exotica — they are integrated into Hand A's general lexicon.

  H1 PASS (0 r-initial) extends the same Basque phonotactic constraint observed in H-BV-BASQUE-PHONOTACTIC-01's stripped-stem analysis. The absence of r-initial headers is not surprising given the general Hand A corpus also has 0.27% r-initial — but it's consistent.

  INTEGRATED INTERPRETATION:
    The headers ARE structurally compatible with a Basque-like vocabulary on the dimensions where direct comparison is possible (phonotactics, length, Hand-A integration). The two failures (H3, H4) are each independently interpretable:
    - H4's low cluster count suggests the 'headers' are morphological variants of a small root set (agglutinative-consistent).
    - H3's low vowel-final rate suggests the headers are case-marked forms, not bare nouns (also agglutinative-consistent).
    Under the Basque-like+Latin-abbreviation hypothesis, both 'failures' are EXPECTED rather than anomalous — but they were pre-registered as phonotactic incompatibilities so they formally fail.
    Honest net: CONFIRMED under locked rule (3/5), but the PASS verdict reflects partial and structural fit, not a clean match to a prototypical Basque content-noun vocabulary.

  LIMITATION: Without a 15th-c. Basque medical/herbal corpus, lexical comparison of these specific headers against real Basque herbal vocabulary is impossible. Test can only rule out STRUCTURAL incompatibility; it cannot positively identify these headers as Basque-origin words.

verdict_note: CONFIRMED 3/5 at threshold. H1 (no r-initial), H2 (length 3.07 mean), H5 (100% Hand-A consistency) pass; H3 (7% vowel-final) and H4 (4 clusters, not 8) fail. The H4 failure reveals a 9-member edit-distance cluster containing chor/shor/or/cho/chol/eol/cheol/shol/ol — these are morphological variants of a small underlying root set, not distinct section names. This is CONSISTENT with agglutinative case-marking of a recurring category word rather than standard encyclopedic per-plant headers. H3's consonant-final bias is also consistent with case-marked rather than bare-stem forms. Both 'failures' are morphologically interpretable in a Basque-like framework but formally fail the pre-registered phonotactic predictions. Honest: the match is partial and structural.
findings_for_followup: The 9-member edit-distance-1 cluster (chor/shor/or/cho/chol/eol/cheol/shol/ol) is the most informative output of this test. If these are case-marked variants of a single root, the root itself is likely ~2-3 glyph units (e.g., 'ol' or 'cho' or 'or' as the base). A follow-up: apply further stripping (remove Layer-1 inners beyond what we already stripped) and see if the 9 members collapse to 1-2 root forms. If they do, we have a candidate decipherment anchor.

H5 consistency = 14/14: every header appears elsewhere in Hand A. This means Brain-V's 'header pool' is not a set of specialized section-marker exotica, but ordinary Hand-A vocabulary that preferentially surfaces at paragraph-initial positions with gallows decoration. The headers are the 14 most-recurrent paragraph-initial forms; their exact-token appearance in non-header positions confirms they are general lexicon members.

'cho' appears 58 times as exact token and 183 times with a Layer-2 outer (cho+y=choy, cho+r=chor, cho+ol=chool, etc.). This is a heavily inflected item — in a Basque reading, 'cho' might be a bare stem (absolutive) and its 183 outer-form occurrences could be its case-marked paradigm. If this is right, 'cho' is a Hand-A high-frequency noun root; its illustrations-context in the actual folios would tell us what it means.

Crucially, without 15th-c. Basque medical/herbal vocabulary reference, none of these structural matches prove the words ARE Basque. A Russian-speaking reader would tell you that 'chor' structurally looks like something, but that's not identification. The preprint should frame these tests as STRUCTURAL COMPATIBILITY probes, not as lexical identifications.

The 'aiin' and 'odaiin' singletons (not clustered) are distinctive. 'aiin' appears 85 times in Hand A as an exact token — the most common standalone non-clustered header. It may be functionally different from the other 12 headers; possibly a paragraph-closing or transitional marker rather than a content header.
reasoning: If Hand A is a Basque-like herbal/pharmaceutical text, the recurring paragraph-initial stems are the closest Brain-V can get to a 'vocabulary surface' — they are the section anchors, presumably naming plants, body parts, or operations. Testing whether this small pool is structurally compatible with a Basque content-word profile provides a final cross-check on the Basque-like hypothesis. Honest limitation: without medieval-Basque medical vocabulary, the test can only rule OUT incompatibility; it cannot positively identify the headers as Basque words.
source: Brain-V 2026-04-17 user-directed falsification attempt on Basque-like+Latin-abbreviation convergence hypothesis; test 4 of 4
